title: Heapsort and selection

publication.changeSummary: Language-neutral DSA draft with explicit C/C++/Java/Python mapping for Heapsort and selection; independent algorithm, implementation, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Algorithmic Foundations; August 2023; material applicable to Heapsort and selection
sources.claim: Heapsort builds a max heap and repeatedly moves the root to the shrinking sorted suffix in O(n log n) time and O(1) auxiliary array space.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Heapsort builds a max heap and repeatedly moves the root to the shrinking sorted suffix in O(n log n) time and O(1) auxiliary array space.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: After each root swap, sift-down restores heap order only in the unsorted prefix. Your goal is to implement or trace heapsort and compare it with merge sort and quicksort. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Array representation and operations; Tree terminology and representation; Heapify, insert and extract; Merge sort and quicksort. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Shared concept and invariant
sections.body: Heapsort builds a max heap and repeatedly moves the root to the shrinking sorted suffix in O(n log n) time and O(1) auxiliary array space. This claim is assessed independently from syntax so conceptual evidence can transfer.

sections.heading: C, C++, Java, and Python implementation map
sections.body: In C, tree nodes and heap buffers need explicit lifetime and bounds; C++ can combine RAII nodes with vector heaps; Java uses object references and PriorityQueue contracts; Python uses objects plus heapq's min-heap functions, while the conceptual invariant stays unchanged.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: It is typically unstable and can have poorer locality than quicksort or library hybrids. The draft therefore rejects: Heapsort is stable because equal values keep heap insertion order.

includes: Use heap structure for in-place comparison sorting and repeated extreme selection.
Implement or trace heapsort and compare it with merge sort and quicksort.

In C, tree nodes and heap buffers need explicit lifetime and bounds; C++ can combine RAII nodes with vector heaps; Java uses object references and PriorityQueue contracts; Python uses objects plus heapq's min-heap functions, while the conceptual invariant stays unchanged.
excludes: It is typically unstable and can have poorer locality than quicksort or library hybrids.

outcomes: Implement or trace heapsort and compare it with merge sort and quicksort.

examples.title: Heapsort and selection: language-neutral trace
examples.situation: After each root swap, sift-down restores heap order only in the unsorted prefix.
walkthrough: Step 1 — State the abstract input and invariant: After each root swap, sift-down restores heap order only in the unsorted prefix.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Standard in-place heapsort is not stable; heap swaps can reverse equal elements.
examples.result: The conceptual result follows because Standard in-place heapsort is not stable; heap swaps can reverse equal elements.

examples.title: Heapsort and selection: implementation transfer
examples.situation: In C, tree nodes and heap buffers need explicit lifetime and bounds; C++ can combine RAII nodes with vector heaps; Java uses object references and PriorityQueue contracts; Python uses objects plus heapq's min-heap functions, while the conceptual invariant stays unchanged.

Step 2 — Map storage, library, equality, bounds, ownership, and error rules using: In C, tree nodes and heap buffers need explicit lifetime and bounds; C++ can combine RAII nodes with vector heaps; Java uses object references and PriorityQueue contracts; Python uses objects plus heapq's min-heap functions, while the conceptual invariant stays unchanged.
Step 3 — Retest this boundary in the selected implementation: It is typically unstable and can have poorer locality than quicksort or library hybrids.
examples.result: Concept mastery transfers, while implementation evidence remains language-specific.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Heapsort is stable because equal values keep heap insertion order.
misconceptions.correction: Standard in-place heapsort is not stable; heap swaps can reverse equal elements.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use an invariant or counterexample to explain why this DSA belief fails: Heapsort is stable because equal values keep heap insertion order.
